title: Exotic magnetism and persistent short-range spin correlations in a frustrated honeycomb lattice antiferromagnet
authors: M. Barik, Q. Faure, F. Damay, J. P. Embs, S. Petit, P. Khuntia
abstract: Two-dimensional high-spin bipartite honeycomb networks, where anisotropy, competing exchange interactions, and spin fluctuations interplay, provide an alternative platform to test theoretical models that distinguish between classical and quantum magnetism in the context of emergent many-body phenomena and exotic excitations. Here, we report the crystal structure, magnetization, specific heat, and inelastic neutron scattering measurements of the $S = 5/2$ distorted honeycomb magnet $\mathrm{CaZn_2Fe(PO_4)_3}$. Magnetization measurements reveal dominant antiferromagnetic interactions between the $\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$ ($S = 5/2$) moments. The development and field evolution of a dip in the magnetic susceptibility under an external magnetic field indicate an unconventional field-induced transition, further supported by anomalies observed in magnetization isotherms. Zero-field specific heat measurements show an antiferromagnetic transition at $T_N \approx 1.67 \mathrm{K}$, which evolves under applied magnetic field, suggesting stabilization of a field-induced spin-canted state. Thermodynamic measurements reveal short-range spin correlations above the transition temperature. Inelastic neutron scattering results further corroborate antiferromagnetic ordering, consistent with specific heat data. Spin-wave calculations indicate competing exchange interactions that introduce magnetic frustration, along with weak Ising-like anisotropy. The interplay of competing interactions and anisotropy gives rise to exotic field-induced behavior and places the system in close proximity to a mean-field tricritical point in the $J_2/J_1$--$J_3/J_1$ phase diagram, opening a route to unconventional states in high-spin frustrated honeycomb magnets.
summary: This paper investigates the magnetic properties of the frustrated honeycomb magnet CaZn2Fe(PO4)3. It demonstrates that competing exchange interactions place the system near a tricritical point and that an applied magnetic field induces an exotic spin-canted state with 2D correlations. This work provides a new platform for studying tunable magnetic phases in high-spin systems.
main_problem: Investigating exotic magnetism, field-induced phase transitions, and the interplay of competing exchange interactions and anisotropy in a high-spin (S=5/2) frustrated honeycomb antiferromagnet.
main_result: The study identifies a field-induced spin-canted state and places the material CaZn2Fe(PO4)3 near a mean-field tricritical point in the J1-J2-J3 phase diagram, exhibiting 2D correlations rather than standard 3D BEC.
method: The researchers used a combination of neutron scattering (INS), thermodynamic measurements (specific heat, magnetization, susceptibility), and theoretical modeling via Linear Spin Wave Theory (LSWT) and spin-wave calculations.
model_or_system: The system is CaZn2Fe(PO4)3 (CZFPO), a distorted honeycomb lattice antiferromagnet with S=5/2 Fe3+ ions, modeled by a J1-J2-J3 Heisenberg Hamiltonian with weak Ising-like anisotropy.
key_observables: Néel temperature (TN), Curie-Weiss temperature (theta_CW), magnetic susceptibility, specific heat, magnetization isotherms, and the magnetic excitation gap.
important_parameters: Exchange parameters (J1=2.08 K, J2=0.348 K, J3=0.023 K), anisotropy (D=0.069 K), and the transition temperature TN approx 1.67 K.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of Linear Spin Wave Theory for modeling excitations and uses a mean-field approach to locate the system in the phase diagram.
figures_summary: Figure 1(c) displays a phase diagram of J2/J1 vs J3/J1 for honeycomb antiferromagnets, positioning CZFPO relative to other known materials.
paper_structure: The paper begins with structural characterization, followed by magnetic and thermodynamic measurements, then moves to neutron scattering results, and concludes with theoretical modeling and phase diagram analysis.
why_interesting: The paper explores many-body dynamics and phase stability near a tricritical point, specifically looking at how external magnetic fields can tune the system between different magnetic regimes and dimensionality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Two-phase Rietveld refinement of the XRD pattern of polycrystalline CZFPO recorded at room temperature. The refinement confirms CZFPO as the dominant phase (> 99%) with a minor (≈0.1%) Zn2P2O7 impurity identified by a peak at 29.5◦. The inset illustrates the distorted honeycomb spin lattice with pink, blue and red bonds representing bond lengths of 4.761 ˚A, 5.087 ˚A and 4.790 ˚A, respectively. As the bond lengths are comparable, nearest neighbor, second nearest neighbor and third nearest neighbors for each site are considered identical and represented as J1, J2 and J3. Fig. 3(b) depicts the evolution of the TN with magnetic field extracted from specific heat, consistent with mag- netic susceptibility measurements. The difference in TN in both of these techniques arises from the distinct un- derlying measurement principles inherent to each method [45, 46]. The TN vs. µ0H replicates a dome-like behav- ior, as observed in several frustrated magnets, suggesting the realization of an unconventional field-induced phase [17, 35, 44]. In the present magnet, the behavior may be ascribed to field-induced spin canting mediated by anisotropic interactions, which originates from the com- bined effects of finite single-ion anisotropy of Fe3+ ions
